solutions.solution: Probably true. The passage clearly mentions that the Indian economy is now subject to global pressures (or 'new pressures'). This will definitely put pressure on the private sector because the Indian industries may suffer as a result of this increased competition. But the inference cannot be declared as definitely true because it is a type of forecast and nobody can predict its accuracy.

Definitely false. Those who are into exports have already been competing with foreign companies and hence, for them, there is no change in the prospects. Those who are catering to domestic demand are now open to global competition and hence subject to new pressures.
Data inadequate. No hint of such nature is made anywhere in the passage.
It is likely that the Soviet Union could not have been the member of any body which it so vehemently opposed.

The second sentence of the passage says that the IMF was accused of being a promoter of economic imperialism. But this sentence is preceded by the first sentence, which says that things have changed. So, probably, this change is in the opinion of such countries about IMF.

Definitely false. The third sentence clearly indicates that a communist country used to have economic relations only with another communist country or a third-world country. USA belongs to neither of these categories.

Both the facts are true; one, that the world economic scenario has changed and, two, that the communist countries have changed their stance. But it not stated anywhere in the passage whether the latter is the result because of the former or the former is an effect.
It is not mentioned clearly in the passage whether GATT indeed exploited its host countries, let alone stopping this exploitation.
There is no information to compare Pakistan and India both of which are South Asian nations.

The passage says that in South Asia almost half of the population is illiterate. Now, India is in South Asia. So, even in India, almost half of the population is probably illiterate. (Probably and not definitely.) So, because the passage talks about South Asian region in general and there may be intra-regional variations.) Now, if we add to our knowledge that India has roughly 100 million people, the conclusion is: almost 50 million people are probably illiterate. So, given inference is probably true. Not definitely true because we can't say if the figure is exact.

Definitely true. The last sentence clearly says that South Asian countries face 'gender inequality', as compared to developed nations'. Probably true. The passage clearly says that South Asian nations are poor, but poverty and GDP are not the same thing. Poverty has more to do with per capita income. Thus, a country of 1 million people with $100,000 million GDP because the per capita income in this former is $100,000 while the second it is $1000 per man, although the former has $16 times as much GDP. Hence, the inference is probably true but not definitely so probably false. It is extremely unlikely in a region of 'gender inequality' that of women being objects of 'mental oppression'. The first sentence of the passage hints at it. But no clear-cut statement is made. Hence, probably true but not definitely true. Not I. Test. But they are not likely to be overweight. Hence, the inference is likely to be false. But it is not definitely false in the light of the given facts. Hence, probably false.

Sufficient data is not given. It is 'one of the lowest' in Asia. But this neither supports nor contradicts the inference that it is 'the lowest'. The last sentence of the passage says the same thing. But that sentence itself is not sure: it uses the phrase 'may be'. Hence, the given inference is probably true but not 'definitely true'.
